title: Sorting Quality Metrics: D<sub>p</sub>, GAF, GDF, and DPQ

learningObjectives.description: Compute D_p (path length metric) for a 1D Gaussian ordering and explain what a lower value means for downstream image codec compression efficiency
Distinguish GAF (local grid adjacency fidelity) from GDF (global distance fidelity) as local vs. global quality measures and explain why both are necessary for a complete sorting quality assessment
Explain the DPQ composite metric and use it to rank the output of different sorting algorithms on the same Gaussian set
Identify when torus wrapping improves 2D grid sorting quality and explain why periodic boundary conditions reduce boundary artifacts in the final compressed grid

content:
## Measuring Sorting Quality

To compare sorting algorithms we need quantitative metrics. Three complementary criteria capture different aspects of what makes a sorted grid layout good.

## 1D Sorting: The D<sub>p</sub> Path Metric

For 1D sorting (arranging \(n\) values on a line), the natural quality measure is the **path length** — the sum of distances between consecutive elements:

\[D_p = \left(\frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} d(x_i, x_{i+1})^p\right)^{1/p}\]

where \(d(x_i, x_{i+1})\) is the distance between consecutive elements and \(p \geq 0\).

- **\(p=1\)**: average absolute difference (Manhattan-like)
- **\(p=2\)**: root mean squared difference (Euclidean-like)

Optimal sorting (smallest values next to small values) minimizes \(D_p\) for any \(p > 0\). This fact lets us define a quality score:

\[\text{quality}_{1D} = 1 - \frac{D_{sort}}{D_{opt}}\]

where \(D_{opt}\) is the path length of the globally optimal sort. **Quality = 1** for optimal sorting, **quality = 0** for random permutation.

**Complication for high-dimensional data**: \(D_{opt}\) is trivially computed for 1D scalars (just sort), but for high-dimensional data (RGB triples, 59-dimensional Gaussian attributes), computing the optimal 1D arrangement is the **Traveling Salesman Problem** — NP-hard.

## Torus Sorting and Wrap-Around

For 2D grids, the choice of topology matters. A **non-wrapped** grid has edges — boundary cells have fewer neighbors. A **wrapped** grid (torus topology) has no boundaries, making every cell equivalent. Torus sorting is preferred when the data has no natural boundary (which Gaussian splats do not).

## 2D Grid Quality Metrics

For 2D grids, we need metrics that capture both **global** and **local** quality.

### Grid Adjacency Fidelity (GAF) — Local Quality

\[GAF = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i} \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}(i)|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} d(x_i, x_j)\]

where \(\mathcal{N}(i)\) is the set of 4 grid neighbors (N, E, S, W) of cell \(i\).

GAF measures the **mean distance to immediate neighbors in attribute space**. A perfectly sorted grid would have zero GAF (all neighbors identical). In practice, we normalize and report as the mean neighborhood distance — lower is better.

**GAF captures**: local smoothness, absence of sharp discontinuities, compressibility under local prediction.

### Global Distance Fidelity (GDF) — Global Quality

\[GDF = \frac{\text{corr}(D_{grid}, D_{attr})}{\|D_{grid}\|\|D_{attr}\|}\]

where \(D_{grid}\) is the pairwise distance matrix in grid space (Manhattan distance between cell coordinates) and \(D_{attr}\) is the pairwise distance matrix in attribute space.

GDF measures whether the grid distances **correlate** with attribute distances. A good sorting preserves the global structure: elements that are far apart in attribute space should be far apart in the grid.

**GDF captures**: global structure preservation, usefulness for visual search (finding similar elements in the grid).

### Distance Preservation Quality (DPQ)

DPQ combines both local and global aspects into a single score used for ranking sorting algorithms. It normalizes the measured grid adjacency fidelity against the optimal achievable value.

## Sorted Grids Support Visual Search

Beyond compression, well-sorted grids have a practical benefit: **visual similarity search**. If you display the \(N\) Gaussian attribute vectors as a sorted grid of colored squares, similar attributes cluster together. A user can visually locate similar Gaussians in O(1) time by looking at a neighborhood, rather than searching the full unordered set.

This is analogous to organizing a color palette as a smooth gradient — much easier to find a specific color than in a random arrangement.

terms.name: Generalised path length
terms.desc: A scalar summarising how large the jumps are between consecutive elements in the sorted sequence. Lower D_p means smoother transitions — adjacent elements are more similar — which is exactly what image codecs exploit.

terms.name: Normalisation by sequence length
terms.desc: Averages the contribution over all n−1 consecutive pairs so that D_p is comparable across sequences of different lengths.

terms.name: Powered pairwise distance
terms.desc: Distance between consecutive elements raised to the power p. Larger p penalises occasional large jumps more severely than many small ones — p=2 is Euclidean (RMS), p=1 is mean absolute difference.

terms.name: p-th root
terms.desc: Undoes the power p so the result is in the original distance units. Together with the inner power, this defines the L^p norm of the gap sequence.

terms.name: Order parameter
terms.desc: Controls sensitivity to outlier jumps. p=1 treats all gaps equally; p=2 penalises large gaps quadratically. For sorting quality, p=1 or p=2 are most common.

terms.name: Normalised sorting quality
terms.desc: A score in [0, 1] measuring how close the achieved sort is to the theoretical optimum. 1 = perfectly optimal ordering, 0 = random permutation (worst case).

terms.name: Path length of the actual sort
terms.desc: The D_p value of the ordering produced by the algorithm being evaluated. Lower is better.

terms.name: Optimal path length
terms.desc: The D_p value of the theoretically best possible ordering — trivially computed for 1D scalars (just sort ascending), but NP-hard to find for high-dimensional data like Gaussian attributes.

terms.name: Average over all grid cells
terms.desc: Computes the metric independently for each of the N grid cells then averages, so every cell contributes equally regardless of position.

terms.name: Average over neighbors
terms.desc: Normalises by the number of immediate neighbors (4 for interior cells on a torus, fewer at boundaries on a non-wrapped grid). Makes boundary and interior cells comparable.

terms.name: Sum over grid neighbors
terms.desc: Sums contributions from the N, E, S, W neighbors of cell i in the 2D grid. Only immediate neighbors are considered — GAF is a purely local metric.

terms.name: Attribute-space distance
terms.desc: Distance between the attribute vectors of cells i and j (e.g., Euclidean distance over all 59 Gaussian parameters). Small values mean neighboring grid cells are attributionally similar — smooth, compressible images.

terms.name: Correlation of distance matrices
terms.desc: Pearson or rank correlation between two N×N matrices: grid distances (how far apart two cells are in the 2D layout) and attribute distances (how different their Gaussian parameters are). High correlation means the layout respects global structure.

terms.name: Pairwise grid distance matrix
terms.desc: Entry (i,j) is the Manhattan distance between cell i and cell j in the 2D grid. Purely a function of layout positions — independent of attribute values.

terms.name: Pairwise attribute distance matrix
terms.desc: Entry (i,j) is the distance between the attribute vectors of Gaussians i and j. This is the ground truth of how similar two Gaussians actually are.

terms.name: Normalisation
terms.desc: Divides by the norms of the two distance matrices to produce a value in [−1, 1], analogous to a cosine similarity between the flattened matrices. Values near 1 indicate strong global structure preservation.

references.label: Barthel et al. 2023
references.title: Improved Evaluation and Generation of Grid Layouts using Distance Preservation Quality
